nativeRange.habitat: Coastal reef flats and the upper edge of seaward reef slopes; generally found from the intertidal down to about 10 m, with records to roughly 20 m.
nativeRange.biotope: Algae- and coral-covered shallow tropical reefs with mixed rock and rubble.

decorPreferences: mature live rock with microalgae growth
scattered rocks and rubble offering perches and crevices

diet.notes: Generally accepts a varied diet of finely chopped crustacean flesh, mysis shrimp, and vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, along with frozen herbivore preparations and dried microalgae and blue-green algae. As a combtooth blenny — a group named for the small comb-like teeth used to scrape surfaces — the Ember Blenny also grazes film and filamentous algae from live rock and glass, so a mature tank with a steady natural algae supply tends to suit it best.

avoidWith: other combtooth blennies of similar appearance
highly aggressive or territorial tankmates that out-compete it for perches

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe, 1419x1064. Sharp full-body side view showing the diagnostic red-streaked/reticulated pattern: dark body with bright orange-red vertical bars, red reticulation on the face, red-rimmed eye and head cirri. Best display of the species' key features.

Debra Baker (via GBIF/iNaturalist), 1547x1309. In-situ side view of a whole live individual showing red facial reticulation and red spotting along a dark body; slightly hazy underwater lighting but correctly ID'd.
Elisabeth Morcel, 1200x876. Sharp frontal portrait showing head cirri, red-rimmed eye and faint red facial reticulation; frontal angle so body not fully shown, kept as an extra.

summary: A small combtooth blenny from Indo-Pacific shallow reefs, generally peaceful and reef-safe, that perches on rockwork and grazes algae while also accepting prepared meaty and herbivore foods.
careNotes: The Ember Blenny does best in a mature aquarium with abundant live rock and steady natural microalgae growth, along with scattered rocks and rubble that provide perching points and crevices to retreat into. It is generally kept one per tank; introducing multiple specimens simultaneously has been reported to reduce intra-species territorial conflict, though this is rarely attempted in home aquariums. Males tend to grow larger than females and have been noted to shift coloration during breeding condition. Generally considered reef-safe with corals and invertebrates. Like most combtooth blennies, this species has been known to jump, so a tightly fitted lid is generally advised.
breedingNotes: The Ember Blenny is an egg-laying species that deposits small, adhesive eggs directly on the substrate (demersal spawning). The larvae that hatch are free-swimming and planktonic, drifting through coastal waters before settling. Captive breeding is rarely reported, and rearing larvae through the full metamorphosis to juvenile fish is generally considered difficult in home aquariums.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry reference: scientific name Cirripectes stigmaticus, max 5 in, temp 72-78 F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, min 55 gal, moderate care, peaceful, reef-safe; diet of chopped crustacean flesh, mysis, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, frozen herbivore prep, and micro/blue-green algae.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomy and wild biology cross-check: Family Blenniidae, Order Blenniiformes, max 10 cm SL, Indo-Pacific (Kenya south to Mozambique through the Indian Ocean to the Marshall and Samoan islands), depth 0-20 m (typically <10 m), preferred temperature 25.7-29.3°C (mean 28.5°C). Oviparous with demersal adhesive eggs; facultative air-breathing reported. Common names include Red-streaked blenny and reticulated blenny. Mild cross-source disagreement on size (FB 10 cm SL vs LA 12.7 cm) and temperature range (FB 25.7-29.3°C wild vs LA 22.2-25.6°C aquarium); averaged in the final values.

fish.breedingNotes: Spawns by depositing small adhesive eggs on the substrate. The hatching larvae are planktonic and drift before metamorphosing into juveniles. Captive rearing through metamorphosis is rarely accomplished in home aquariums.
